###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2011-01-01T21:31:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6c2b59b6-6235-46e9-87c5-6c54dc46f3db@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <xu2dne7cMfvJe4rQnZ2dnUVZ_vKdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <fiflh6t8dnnu5qrpn7kpo2eoa27uv66ep3@4ax.com>`

```
Richard Luketich wrote:
> On Sun, 26 Dec 2010 19:05:24 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> multiple ODO in the same structure becomes complex.

If it's not moved, it's already complex. Consider:

Assume My-num = 3, we have.

x x x W . . .

Now
MOVE 4 TO My-Num.
MOVE "Z" TO Mydata-Really (My-Num)

If Wally is not moved, you must have either

x x x Z W . . . (which generates immense run-time complexity)
or
x x x Z . . . (Wally has left the building)

I suggest the only way a field subordinate to an ODO can exist efficiently 
is when the succeeding field exists at a location GREATER than the max of 
the ODO variable, viz:

x x x x . . . . . Wally

Even this might generate funky code when you MOVE the group somewhere. Does 
the MOVE include the implied slack bytes or does the MOVE work like the 
STRING verb?

I'm reminded of a discussion in the Talmud. The tax on a newborn male is 1 
Shekel per head, payable to the Temple priests. The discussion was over what 
must the tax be if a TWO-HEADED child was born? Was the tax one Shekel or 
two?

The whole business was put to rest when one rabbi, evidently wiser than the 
rest, said "Put the child under a cherem (banished as impure) and the 
problem goes away."

The analogy here is obvious.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2011-01-03T01:52:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ifra4u$chb$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<6c2b59b6-6235-46e9-87c5-6c54dc46f3db@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <xu2dne7cMfvJe4rQnZ2dnUVZ_vKdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <fiflh6t8dnnu5qrpn7kpo2eoa27uv66ep3@4ax.com> <6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
In article <6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>Richard Luketich wrote:
>> On Sun, 26 Dec 2010 19:05:24 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
>> wrote:

[snip]

>>> Imagine
>>> 01  Mydata.
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>STRING verb?

I am not a compiler author nor am I familiar with compiler intracies but 
it would make sense if Wally were placed at the outermost bound of the ODO 
field which depends on it.  Were the ODO field to change in size then 
either slack bytes out to its limits would have to be preserved at compile 
or *all* the WS fields after it would be subject to movement as the 
'depending' value changes.

How a MOVE of such a field works might be readily determined by compiling 
something with a PMAP... errr, LIST or other Assembley-generating 
equivalent.

>
>I'm reminded of a discussion in the Talmud. The tax on a newborn male is 1 
>Shekel per head, payable to the Temple priests.

I'm reminded of the Wife of Bath making things up whole cloth and 
supporting it with 'And you can look it up in your Almagest'.  The 
redemption of the firstborn, as best I recall it, is based on Ex.XIII.2 
and the sum specifically mentioned in Num.III.47 is five shekels (100 
gerahs).

Do you have a cite for this 'Talmudic discussion' or did it occur around 
the place where it is discussed what happens when two men fall down a 
chimmney and one comes out clean while the other is dirty?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2011-01-03T18:28:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ift4ga$ae1$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<6c2b59b6-6235-46e9-87c5-6c54dc46f3db@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <ifra4u$chb$1@reader1.panix.com> <waCdnWD7VNXCd7zQnZ2dnUVZ_qydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
In article <waCdnWD7VNXCd7zQnZ2dnUVZ_qydnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
HeyBub <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jewish equivalent of an old-wives-tale, or b) that I make this shit up.

I made no assumptions and explicitly stated that I was reminded of 
something; last time I looked in the United States of America one was 
still permitted a bit of freedom of association.

>
>The discussion on the two-headed child can be found in (Babylonian) Menahot 
>37a-b. 

This is a difficult often encountered with languages with other alphabets; 
the 'h' in Menahot might be a soft h (Hebrew alphabet 'heh' or 'hay') or 
an aspirated 'h' (Hebrew alphabet 'chet' or 'ches', where 'ch' is 
pronounced as in the Scottish 'loch').  According to 
http://www.mediafire.com/file/ytnetzdmjno/Menachoth.pdf this reads:

--begin quoted text:

[37a]

In the meantime there came a man [to the school] saying, I have begotten a 
first-born child with two heads, how much must I give the priest?11 An old 
man came forward and ruled that he must give [the priest] ten sela's. But 
this is not so! For Rami b. Hama learnt: From the verse. The firstborn of 
man thou shalt surely redeem,12 I might conclude that this would apply 
even when the firstborn was rendered trefah13 within thirty days [of his 
birth]. Scripture therefore added,

[37b]

Howbeit,1 limiting thereby [the general application]!2  In this case it is 
different since the Divine Law declared [the law of redemption] to be 
governed by the expression per head.3

--end quoted text

Now, should this leave any doubts to even the most occluded eye note that 
footnote 3 for 37b reads '(3) Num. III, 47. Consequently as this child has 
two heads and is now living there must be a payment of ten sela's for his
redemption.'

It is left to the reader to determine whether the aforementioned (but 
clipped from this post) declaration of impurity was 'the Jewish equivalent 
of an old-wives-tale', fabricated whole cloth, misread, misremembered or 
the ever-popular E) Some of the Above.

DD
```

```
condition of being-reminded (associating).  The mechanisms and reasons for 
memory are, according to those who have studied such things, 
ill-understood and subject for marvel... did you know that memories can be 
recalled during surgery simply by touching a portion of the brain?

>
>You said [implicitly] that my post reminded you of the Wife of Bath 
>inventing stories, presumably for entertainment.

Please read carefully.  I said *explicitly* that I was reminded of the 
Wife of Bath's 'And you can look it up in your Almagest'.  I then 
explicitly asked for a citation; your presumptions are your own.

>By extension, my 
>observation seemingly had some sort of equivalence, in your mind, with 
>on-the-fly fiction.

By extension a mouse's neck is the equivalent of a giraffe's because they 
contain the same number of bones.  *I* barely know what goes on in my mind 
and I wonder at the accuracy of other folks' finding stuff in there such 
as 'equivalence' and 'presumption'.

subject to redemption fees whereas in the citation I gave (and link to

newborn is considered subject to double-fees.

You stated the text said zero tax.  It is pointed out, with URLs, that the 
text states double tax.  Whom do you expect to admit error?

>
>In the future, if something seems odd to you, I suggest a simple "That

>engender ill-will than meanderings that decode as: "You'd lie when the truth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Bill Klein" <wmklein@noreply.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2011-01-03T14:39:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cuqUo.86591$Ud7.16395@en-nntp-16.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<6c2b59b6-6235-46e9-87c5-6c54dc46f3db@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <xu2dne7cMfvJe4rQnZ2dnUVZ_vKdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <fiflh6t8dnnu5qrpn7kpo2eoa27uv66ep3@4ax.com> <6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <ifra4u$chb$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ifra4u$chb$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <6OCdnXNcg73pbILQnZ2dnUVZ_gSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
> HeyBub <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>
 What you describe (placing the item AFTER the ODO) is what happens with 
Micro Focus with NO-ODOSLIDE specified, i.e. the maxium size of the table is 
always "allocated".  SEARCH (and subscript checking) is based on the CURRENT 
size of the item, but the other items are allcoated after or with the 
maximum size.

This is not how any current (or past) IBM compiler works.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

- **From:** pakku <pakku@artlover.com>
- **Date:** 2011-01-03T13:34:06-08:00

```
Thanks all who replied.  I followed the link to the Cobol Language reference on Complex ODO which in turn pointed me to Appendix 2 of the Cobol Programming guide.  I can see where he talks about my situation (Bullet 3: "Table that has variable-length elements: A data item described by an OCCURS clause contains a subordinate data item describled by an OCCURS clause with the DEPENDING ON phrase")
But I couldn't determine what I was supposed to do different to handle this.

This is a snippet from the Compiler Listings that has the error message.  Due to the length limitations I'm afraid it's all wrapped around rather confusingly.

    01  LK-RRBF021.                                                     
        05  LK-COMM-SEND-TEST-OCCURS   PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.             
                                                                        
        05  LK-COMM-TEST-AREA  OCCURS 5 TIMES.                          
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-TEST-RET-CODE          PIC X(02).           
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS           PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.   
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN      OCCURS 500 TIMES         
                             DEPENDING ON LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS        
 ==000687==> IGYGR1263-S "OCCURS DEPENDING ON" object "LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS" was defined as a 
                         table element.  The "DEPENDING ON" phrase was discarded. 
 ==000687==> IGYGR1116-S The "DEPENDING ON" object for table "LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN" was 
0                        invalid.  The "DEPENDING ON" phrase was discarded. 
                                   INDEXED BY LK-COMM-CPT-INDX.           
                15  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-CODE           PIC X(06).
```

```
OK- now I begin to understand what the problem is.  I didn't read the error message closely enough.  In the example in the programming guide, the DEPENDING ON was on a variable declared OUTSIDE the higher level OCCURS. (Like Frank suggested doing).

My situation is like so- Each Test is broken down into a different number of CPT-Codes, i.e the data store has a different number of children rows for each parent row.
I read the database and find that Test(1) has 4 CPTs
So I'd like to populate LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS(1) with 4 and then create 4 occurrences of LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN below that
Test(2) has 10 CPTs
Then I'd like to populate LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS(2) =10 and 10 occurrences of LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN below that and so on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-06T02:11:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oj901FetmU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6f1a9e6a-1383-4252-9bfc-e7a605aae74f@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <ig081p$kso$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article
> <6f1a9e6a-1383-4252-9bfc-e7a605aae74f@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com>,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> children rows for each parent row.

This describes a data structure commonly referred to as a "node". Techniques 
for processing nodes are pretty standard and generally involve recursion of 
the children.

Here's a description in English:

1. Access  a node root.

2. Access each child of that root  and process it, until no more children 
are returned. (if there are children of each child, recurse through them in 
exactly the same way, treating the current child as a "temporary root" and 
using the same process code. Repeat this to as many levels as there are 
children of children.

3. Move to the next node root.

4. Continue doing this until all roots (or the ones you are interested in, 
if you are accessing them randomly or filtering them) have been processed.


A very typical use of node structures would be in building a Treeview...

You don't know how many "branches" there may be at each level, and it could 
vary... (Trees don't have to be "balanced"...)

Once you recognise the structure and the recursive nature of the processing, 
you can see that there is absolutely NO NEED FOR OCCURS DEPENDING ON at 
all...

(The only exception to this would be where some deluded idiot has already 
created the structure as an ODO, instead of a normailzed set of repeating 
groups or node collection. If you have control over it, or it exists on a 
DB, there is NO reason at all to use ODO, and there are actually a good 
number of reasons NOT to do so... see other posts in this thread.)

Apart from consideration of implementations of ODO varying on different 
platforms, ANY ODO solution simply introduces complexity and cumbersome data 
storage management to what is essentially a simple and recognised data 
structure (NODE).

>> I read the database and find that Test(1) has 4 CPTs

"Test" is a table and CPT is a linked table?

CPTs are connected to "Test" by a foreign key, right? (A standard "repeating 
group" on a relational database. This supports any number of CPTs per 
"Test")

You have decided you want all the connected rows in a single data structure, 
defined in COBOL.   In fact, the data is already available in a normalized 
form (The repeating group (CPT) has been written to a separate table joined 
on a foreign key back to Test. (This constitutes 1st Normal form, and, 
coincidentally,also complies with 2nd and 3rd Normal form, because the key 
used to link the tables is unique). You could get what you appear to be 
wanting with a simple SQL Query:

SELECT  TestArea, CPTCode
FROM TestTable x, CPTTable y
WHERE x.KEY = y.KEY
GROUP BY TestArea

(Or somethng similar... there are minor differences in SQL depending on your 
RDBMS)

This should list the Test Areas with the CPTs for each one, irrespective of 
the number of them.



>> So I'd like to populate LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS(1) with 4 and then
>> create 4 occurrences of LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN below that.

If you are really determined to have this in a COBOL data structure you are 
setting yourself a difficult task.

Here's the data structure you posted:

    01  LK-RRBF021.
        05  LK-COMM-SEND-TEST-OCCURS   PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.

        05  LK-COMM-TEST-AREA  OCCURS 5 TIMES.
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-TEST-RET-CODE          PIC X(02).
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS           PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN      OCCURS 500 TIMES
                             DEPENDING ON LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS
 ==000687==> IGYGR1263-S "OCCURS DEPENDING ON" object 
"LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS" was defined as a
                         table element.  The "DEPENDING ON" phrase was 
discarded.
 ==000687==> IGYGR1116-S The "DEPENDING ON" object for table 
"LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN" was
0                        invalid.  The "DEPENDING ON" phrase was discarded.
                                   INDEXED BY LK-COMM-CPT-INDX.
                15  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-CODE           PIC X(06).

It is ugly, limited (500 occurrences hard coded in it), and unnecessary.

If your compiler doesn't support standard actions against NODE collections 
(Fujitsu does...), then you COULD build a "record" containing a given Test 
with its corresponding CPTs but you WOULDN'T  use ODO.

Instead you would recognise root and child nodes and use STRING to add them 
to a single "record" which is really just a big block (and can be defined as 
a variable length record in COBOL without any need for OCCURS...)

Consider this:

(WORKING-STORAGE)

    01  LK-COMM-TEST-AREA.  *> root node
            10  LK-NODE-KEY              (whatever field(column) is used to 
join the Test and CPT tables on the RDB.)
            10  LK-COMM-RTN-TEST-RET-CODE          PIC X(02).

    01  LK-COMM-CPT.  *> child node.
                  15  LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-CODE           PIC X(06).



(FILE SECTION)

01 LK-OUT-RECORD   PIC X(32000) <or whatever...> SELECT  NODE-FILE has it as 
VARYING FROM 1 TO 32000.


(PROCEDURE DIVISION)

(Get the Test row from the DB)
...

  INITIALIZE-NODE-OUTPUT.
           MOVE SPACES TO LK-OUT-RECORD.

 CREATE-NODE-FILE.

           PERFORM PROCESS-NODES  UNTIL DB-FINISHED
           WRITE LK-OUT-RECORD
           CLOSE NODE-FILE

PROCESS-NODES.
           PERFORM STRING-ROOT-NODE
           PERFORM STRING-CHILD-NODE UNTIL  DB-CPT-FINISHED
           ...


STRING-ROOT-NODE.
           (GET "TEST" ROW FROM DB INTO LK-COMM-TEST-AREA ...)
           IF NOT DB-FINISHED
                STRING LK-OUT-RECORD
                                DELIMITED BY SPACE
                          LK-COMM-TEST-AREA
                                DELIMITED BY SPACE
                          (ADD A "NODE DELIMITER" CHARACTER HERE IF YOU WANT 
ONE...)
                                        INTO LK-OUT-RECORD
               END-STRING
          END-IF
         ...

STRING-CHILD-NODE.
           (GET "CPT" ROW FROM DB INTO LK-COMM-CPT...      Check for a 
change of key on the CPT rows as well as physical end)
           IF NOT CPT-FINISHED
                STRING LK-OUT-RECORD
                                DELIMITED BY SPACE
                          LK-COMM-CPT
                                DELIMITED BY SPACE
                          (ADD A "NODE DELIMITER" CHARACTER HERE IF YOU WANT 
ONE...)
                                        INTO LK-OUT-RECORD
               END-STRING
          END-IF
         ...


The above is VERY basic code and is designed to illustrate a concept, not to 
be implemented.

Having considered the very basic NODE processing above, it might occur to 
you that this is just a pale imitation of XML processing.

XML is a NODE structure and perfectly fits your requirement.

If I were you, I 'd read the Database and provide the NODE structure as an 
XML file. This is transportable across platforms and can be encoded and 
decoded using existing standard software, that can be called from COBOL. An 
XML handler will automatically encode your nodes so that they can be read by 
any XML editor. If a report is required it can be produced using standard 
tools like Crystal Reports, specifying the XML file as the data source.

The LAST thing you need is COBOL ODO.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** pakku <pakku@artlover.com>
- **Date:** 2011-01-05T13:33:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c2c5ecf1-d9fa-48cc-88fa-d8268b473ebe@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8oj901FetmU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Thanks Pete for a detailed note on the best way to do this.  Your surmise about the data structure is correct.
I have some constraints which might prevent me from doing this the optimal way.
a) There exists a subroutine that gets me the CPT codes for a Test.  The process of going from Test to CPT is not that straightforward hence a subroutine for this purpose.  I have to use this.
b) This subroutine has a linkage section that is defined as capable of accepting 1 to 75 tests using ODO and returning a fixed 500 CPTs per test.   
05  LK-SEND-TEST-OCCURS               PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
05  LK-TEST-AREA  OCCURS 1 TO 75 TIMES
                  DEPENDING ON LK-SEND-TEST-OCCURS
                  INDEXED BY LK-TEST-INDX.
    10  LK-RTN-TEST-PRICE             PIC S9(5)V99 COMP-3.
    10  LK-RTN-CPT-OCCURS             PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
    10  LK-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN      OCCURS 500 TIMES
                                  INDEXED BY LK-CPT-INDX.

c) I am writing a Web Service using CICS that will call this subroutine.  If the Interface Area for the Web Service Cobol Program that calls this  subroutine were the same format as the subroutine's own linkage section, then the XML file that is sent back to the client is going to be unnecessarily huge since it's going to have a bunch of "empty" entries.

So to answer Richard's question- I'm not trying to save memory here, but trying to minimise the amount of data being sent back to the client.

d) If I set up an interface copybook for just 1 Test-Area with the CPT-Breakdown occuring from 1 to 500 then the returned XML document doesn't have any "empty" entries.
e) I was hoping to be able to do the same but with more than 1 Test-Area, the 5 was just a random choice.  I could have used 75 if I wanted to.

If I used STRING and created a long record of CPT Breakdowns for each Test, then wouldn't I have to parse it and add back the tags for XML?
Right now the Web Service tools at my disposal save me the trouble of creating an XML document.  The tools use the OCCURS clauses in the interface copybook to create the CPT Breakdown elements in the XML.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-07T03:04:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8om0gdFj1qU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<c2c5ecf1-d9fa-48cc-88fa-d8268b473ebe@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com>`

```
pakku wrote:
> Thanks Pete for a detailed note on the best way to do this.  Your
> surmise about the data structure is correct.

Good. The main reason I responded to you was because you explained your 
problem well (with examples) and because I REALLY HATE ODO!

(Not that I would ever get emotional about computer code, you understand... 
;-))

> I have some constraints which might prevent me from doing this the
> optimal way.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> per test.

Ah, I see...(So the calling program has the same structure defined in WS or 
ITS LS?)

> 05  LK-SEND-TEST-OCCURS               PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
> 05  LK-TEST-AREA  OCCURS 1 TO 75 TIMES
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>           15 LK-RTN-CPT                       PIC X(6).

This is the exception I referred to previously. You are stuck with what has 
already been done. However, it is not as black as it looks... (To be fair, 
this may be the only way it can be done in COBOL; I honestly don't know, 
because I'm not dealing with CICS web services.)

The LINKAGE Section actually occupies no space at all. This suggests a 
solution outlined below...


> c) I am writing a Web Service using CICS that will call this
> subroutine.  If the Interface Area for the Web Service Cobol Program
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> bunch of "empty" entries.

Yes, you're absolutely right about the emptiness, but the overall size is 
not SO bad. It will be (75 x 6)  = 450  + (75 x 500 x 6) = 225000... 
er...lots

In context, you probably wouldn't hesitate to attach a 225K jpg to an email 
and send it over the net...

Still, as I'm writing web services quite a lot lately I understand your 
point and I would be looking hard for ways to make it smaller, just as you 
are...
>
> So to answer Richard's question- I'm not trying to save memory here,
> but trying to minimise the amount of data being sent back to the
> client.

A noble aspiration :-)

I take it "the XML being sent back to the client" is SOAP? As far as I know 
there is no way to provide a variable length SOAP field other than with a 
string, or by manipulating the WSDL (there are MAX and MIN tags available). 
BUT, I would expect the code writing the SOAP to NOT write "empty" entries. 
In fact, it should suppress generation (including tags) altogether if it has 
nothing to say...

It's a long time (30 years :-)) since I used CICS but I understand that it 
now contains routines which allow it to generate SOAP/WSDL for web services. 
Depending how comprehensive these facilities are, it should be possible to 
tell the routine that the generated XML is not to have tags generated for 
"empty" entries and that variable strings are not to be padded.

Have a read of this: 
http://www-01.ibm.com/support/docview.wss?uid=swg21248612

If it is a bit too deep see if you can get into the Web Service and see how 
it talks to CICS


> d) If I set up an interface copybook for just 1 Test-Area with the
> CPT-Breakdown occuring from 1 to 500 then the returned XML document
> doesn't have any "empty" entries.

Yes, that is what I would expect. There is only one variable field and the 
WSDL generated by CICS knows not to pad it. When you add more occurrences of 
TEST, the block being passed is larger (because ODO always allocates the 
maximum size). What you need is to get it closer to a "dynamic allocation" 
(COBOL is hopeless at this, but CICS isn't...)


> e) I was hoping to be able to do the same but with more than 1
> Test-Area, the 5 was just a random choice.  I could have used 75 if I
> wanted to.

In fact, you actually WANT 75 as an ODO :-)

I have a possible solution to this but don't beat me up if it doesn't 
work... :-) I'm not working in a mainframe environment so I can't test it.

If I understand correctly you have a COBOL Web Service which calls a 
subroutine and gets back the block you have defined. The subroutine doesn't 
generate the XML, does it? It is the program which calls it?

The WSDL for the service defines the shape of the XML and you are correct 
that if it just uses the same interface as was passed to the subroutine, the 
data transmitted over the wire will be too large.

Presumably, you can control the SOAP, as this is being generated in the 
program which calls the subroutine? The SOAP has to comply with the WSDL 
used to define the data transfer (XML). Probably you generate the WSDL using 
CICS and passing it the interface block definition? (I'm not sure about this 
because CICS never had this facility when I was programming mainframes.) 
This, in effect, defines your Web Service.

If any of this is wrong, please clarify. Given that it is so (or generally 
correct), here's a thought...

Although the SOAP is being generated currently from the same interface block 
that was passed to the CPT subroutine, it actually doesn't HAVE to be.

Why not define the interface in the calling program exactly as it is defined 
in the subroutine (always a good move and it guarantees no interface 
problems), but DON'T pass this interface down the wire to the client?

Instead define a more dynamic structure as follows:

(WORKING-STORAGE)

01 SOAP-STRUCTURE.
 05  SOAP-SEND-TEST-OCCURS               PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
 05  SOAP-TEST-AREA  OCCURS 1 TO 75 TIMES
                   DEPENDING ON WS-TEST-COUNT
                   INDEXED BY SOAP-TEST-INDX.
     10  SOAP-RTN-TEST-PRICE             PIC S9(5)V99 COMP-3.
     10  SOAP-RTN-CPT-OCCURS             PIC S9(03)   COMP-3.
     10  SOAP-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN      OCCURS 500 TIMES
                   DEPENDING ON WS-CPT-COUNT
                   INDEXED BY SOAP-CPT-INDX.
           15   SOAP-RTN-CPT   PIC X(6).

01  WS-TEST-COUNT  PIC S9(3) COMP-3.
01  WS-CPT-COUNT    PIC S9(3) COMP-3.

01  WS-CPT-MAX    PIC S9(3) COMP-3   VALUE ZERO.

01   WS-SOAP-PTR USAGE POINTER.
01   WS-SOAP-LENGTH   PIC S9(8) BINARY.

01    SUBSCRIPTS USAGE COMP-3.
        12 J   PIC S9(3).
        12 K  PIC S9(3).
...

Make sure the WSDL is generated from the SOAP-STRUCTURE, above.

Now "all ya gotta do is"   load THIS structure  with the data from the 
interface block passed to the CPT subroutine, and then send it down the 
wire. Because this needs to be a dynamic structure it would make sense to 
move it to LINKAGE (where it actually occupies no space,) ask CICS to 
allocate a chunk of memory to it, and set the address of that chunk of 
memory as what you pass down the line to the Client.

Summarising:

1. Call the CPT routine using the "large" definition it is expecting. It 
returns into the same area. (WS in the calling program).
2.  Load the SOAP-STRUCTURE in WS of the calling program from the interface 
block returned by the CPT subroutine.

Here's some sample code:

(PROCEDURE DIVISION)

LOAD-SOAP-STRUCTURE.
        PERFORM
                VARYING SOAP-TEST-INDX
                         FROM 1
                               BY 1
                         UNTIL  SOAP-TEST-INDX >  LNK-SND-TEST-OCCURS
                 AFTER    LK-CPT-INDX
                         FROM 1
                               BY 1
                         UNTIL  LK-RTN-CPT (LK-CPT-INDX) =  SPACES
                              *> SYNCHRONIZE THE 2 STRUCTURES

                               SET J TO SOAP-TEST-INDX
                               SET LK-TEST-INDX TO J
                               SET J TO LK-CPT-INDX
                               SET SOAP-CPT-INDX TO J

                              IF WS-CPT-MAX < SOAP-CPT-INDX
                                         SET WS-CPT-MAX TO SOAP-CPT-INDX
                              END-IF

                              *> SET THE SOAP DEPENDENCY VALUES

                              SET WS-TEST-COUNT TO SOAP-TEST-INDX
                              SET  WS-CPT-COUNT TO SOAP-CPT-INDX

                              *> MOVE THE DATA

                               MOVE LK-RTN-CPT (LK-TEST-INDX, LK-CPT-INDX) 
TO
                                            SOAP-RTN-CPT (SOAP-TEST-INDX, 
SOAP-CPT-INDX)
          END-PERFORM
          *> SET THE MAXIMUM CPT OCCURRENCE VALUE THAT WAS ENCOUNTERED
          *> INTO THE DYNAMIC SOAP STRUCTURE. THE MAXIMUM TEST VALUE  IS
          *> ALREADY  SET...

          MOVE  WS-CPT-MAX   TO WS-CPT-COUNT

          *> THE SOAP STRUCTURE IS NOW LOADED DYNAMICALLY AND CAN BE
          *> SENT TO THE CLIENT

3.  Move the SOAP-STRUCTURE to the LINKAGE SECTION. (It takes no space). On 
reflection, it would probably be OK to define the SOAP-STRUCTURE in LINKAGE 
SECTION, but the WS fields for ODO must be in WS. Use the LENGTH function to 
find out how long it is...

          MOVE LENGTH OF SOAP-STRUCTURE TO WS-SOAP-LENGTH

4. Ask CICS for some memory for it. (GETMAIN)

          EXEC CICS GETMAIN
               BELOW
               USERDATAKEY
               SET(WS-SOAP-PTR)
               FLENGTH(WS-SOAP-LENGTH)
         END-EXEC
         SET ADDRESS OF SOAP-STRUCTURE TO  WS-SOAP-PTR

That should give you a pseudo dynamic structure that will support as many 
TESTs as there actually were and the maximum number of CPTs actually 
encountered, for each TEST, rather than the fixed 500. (So it still isn't 
perfect because it can never be truly dynamic as long as it is based on 
ODO). I do something similar in C# and it is EXACTLY as large as it needs to 
be... COBOL is simply not intended for the Network, and trying to make it do 
Network stuff by fudging it with CICS and ODO is like putting lipstick on a 
pig.

Don't forget to issue CICS FREEMAIN once you have sent it :-)

> If I used STRING and created a long record of CPT Breakdowns for each
> Test, then wouldn't I have to parse it and add back the tags for XML?

Probably... I'm not sure because I don't know whether you write your own 
WSDL or CICS is doing it. I think we can safely discard that solution 
anyway.

> Right now the Web Service tools at my disposal save me the trouble of
> creating an XML document.  The tools use the OCCURS clauses in the
> interface copybook to create the CPT Breakdown elements in the XML.

OK, that sounds fine.

I have taken more time than I really should have to do this because it is an 
interesting exercise in COBOL and it gives me a break from some of the other 
stuff I am currently grappling with. :-)

I hope it is of some help.

Pete.
```

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8oj901FetmU1@mid.individual.net> <c2c5ecf1-d9fa-48cc-88fa-d8268b473ebe@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 5 Jan 2011 13:33:41 -0800 (PST), pakku <pakku@artlover.com>
wrote:

>Thanks Pete for a detailed note on the best way to do this.  Your surmise about the data structure is correct.
>I have some constraints which might prevent me from doing this the optimal way.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Right now the Web Service tools at my disposal save me the trouble of creating an XML document.  The tools use the OCCURS clauses in the interface copybook to create the CPT Breakdown elements in the XML.

From this posting, I assume this project is for something running on
an IBM z series under zOS.  If so the current COBOL compiler has
significant XML capability an the next most current still had a fair
amount of it.  Rather than fiddling with OCCURS DEPENDING ON, I would
look at the XML tools available in the shop.  For the internal
processing just use the maximum size tables needed with counters that
tell the actual number of entries used.

Clark Morris
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-04T15:34:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20a666d4-0ca5-493f-8330-ccdb2f4174f4@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6f1a9e6a-1383-4252-9bfc-e7a605aae74f@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jan 5, 11:17 am, pakku <pa...@artlover.com> wrote:
> OK- now I begin to understand what the problem is.  I didn't read the error message closely enough.  In the example in the programming guide, the DEPENDING ON was on a variable declared OUTSIDE the higher level OCCURS. (Like Frank suggested doing).
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then I'd like to populate LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-OCCURS(2) =10 and 10 occurrences of LK-COMM-RTN-CPT-BREAKDOWN below that and so on.

A decade or three ago there may have been a point to doing it that
way: saving disk space on a 20MegaByte drive, for example, or being
limited in the number of files that can be opened.

What assurance do you have that there are not more CPT than you have
allowed for in your OCCURS ? Or indeed that there are not more tests
than in the higher OCCURS ?

Why are you not storing the data in a normalised form with separate
files for the tests and the CPTS each keyed adequate to relate them ?
It would seem to me to be far less complicated and could cater for
almost unlimited tests, and it has the bonus of working.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested "Occurs Depending On"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-05T23:56:13+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oj12fFurkU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6f1a9e6a-1383-4252-9bfc-e7a605aae74f@glegroupsg2000goo.googlegroups.com> <20a666d4-0ca5-493f-8330-ccdb2f4174f4@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com> <ig0hcp$k4n$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article
> <20a666d4-0ca5-493f-8330-ccdb2f4174f4@e16g2000pri.googlegroups.com>,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Disproving logical negatives can be a dicey thing, Mr Plinston.

So can proving them, but, if the need arises... :-)
>
>>
>> Why are you not storing the data in a normalised form with separate
>> files for the tests and the CPTS each keyed adequate to relate them ?

This is an infinitely superior "generic" solution than trying to map 
OCCURrences.

Given that the technology for normalized repeating groups of ANY size is 
readily available, I too have difficulty in understanidng why anyone would 
want to use a stulted solution like ODO (which is arguably obsolete, never 
at any time delivered what programmers hoped it would (a dynamically 
allocated variable length array in COBOL), and, at least in my book, is 
"best avoided". To consider nested ODO, even if it is technically available, 
just seems like compounding what is a dreadful solution to start with.

>
> These may be wild guesses, Mr Plinston, but the answer might be along
> the lines of 'the specs don't allow for that' or 'the person who
> signs my timesheet wants to see it this way'.

The specs. allow for whatever solves the problem, unless they are being 
specified to the level of  program code. (It has been many years since this 
level of specification was considered a "Good Thing"; the person writing the 
spec. might as well write the code and thereby ensure that it is a perfect 
match to one person's pre-conceived idea of what constitutes "good" COBOL 
code.) I believe the spec. is functional, rather than program code level.

I further believe that the "person who signs the OP's timesheet" wants a 
solution.

A quick review of the posts in this thread shows that using ODO in COBOL is 
simply a poor solution.

Using normalized repeating groups is a much better one and you can still 
implement it in COBOL if you are desperate to do that.


Pete.
```

- **References:** `<ca26d265-1a5f-429b-a7c9-d05ebf46599e@p7g2000prb.googlegroups.com> <eec0f226-5862-449d-8cef-330dd1169c03@f21g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <ef7bddb7-0661-4690-8bba-fd0e55ba5f7c@m20g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <5513188f-6742-433b-951c-be4bc4cc497a@c13g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <2cbc2916-6d3a-48a1-b160-600ab11714c8@h17g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <80baf431-9fd8-4e69-9884-cc598c2a018e@v17g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jan 3, 3:11 pm, iNFO_rene <infodynamics...@yahoo.com> wrote:
> > > The objective is;
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> far I already did that, except the downloading and printing.

So why not use a COBOL 'CALL "SYSTEM"' to run wget ?

          STRING "wget "   DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 URL-site  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 filename  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 x"00"     DELIMITED BY SIZE
              INTO WGet-Command
          CALL "SYSTEM" USING WGet-Command

          OPEN INPUT Text-File
          ...

Of course you will also need to install wget along with your program.


> Well, after seeing the Python command you gave.... I think I am really
> looking differently on solutions to a much simpler Python command.
>
> Thanks Richard :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT Re: Aftershock

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-05T23:30:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8oivieFmb5U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<a99836f1-f94b-4d8f-81de-2cb4375031d0@l7g2000vbv.googlegroups.com> <8nqll4FimgU1@mid.individual.net> <8o0qj3Fi6nU1@mid.individual.net> <10ac227c-cf35-4543-9e11-ff53365f78f3@f9g2000vbq.googlegroups.com>`

```
PR wrote:
> On Dec 29 2010, 7:15 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[127 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul

Thanks Paul, that is very kind. I don't think a tripStateside is on the 
cards just at the moment as a have a fair bit going on at the moment. But I 
do appreciate the offer, always enjoy visiting the USA and will bear it in 
mind for the future. Similarly, I'd be happy to see any CLC regulars who 
decided to holiday "Down Under", and I have a guest room. :-)

It really is very beautiful here and, at the moment, we are having an 
incredible Summer.

Today I spent the whole day with friends at a remote beach on the Coromandel 
peninsula. I had never been there before, and it was simply breathtaking. I 
like driving, and 2 hours in an open car over winding roads through some 
simply unbelievable scenery is a very pleasant way to spend time. My friends 
have a 17 foot small cabin cruiser which they use for SCUBA diving and water 
skiing (there is a Marine Park underwater, just offshore). In about 10 
minutes they took 90 scallops, but the quota is 20 per person per day so the 
60 largest were retained and the rest returned to the ocean.. We had them 
for lunch, and I can confirm that when they are within an hour of being 
caught, they taste completely different from the store-bought variety. :-).

It was suggetsed I stay overnight but I have some commitments at home 
tomorrow so we dined at a local restaurant (absolutely excellent food and 
very reasonably priced) and I left at around 8:00 pm. The roads which were 
moderately congested during the day were almost deserted at night and I 
drove home through the Summer evening, arriving just before 10:00pm. The 
warm night air with the roof down and the stars above is another dimension 
on a great experience.

Here's a link to the beach I was at. It is simply stunnning...

http://www.hahei.co.nz/index.html

Fortunately, there have been no further detectable tremors in the Bay or at 
Christchurch, so, hopefully, things are settling down a bit.

Pete.
```

```
I was born in Wellington so have a soft spot for our Capital, although I 
haven't been there for a long time.

Wellington, of course, is built on a fault line and has earthquakes all the 
time...
```

```
Steve,

if it comes off for you, see if you can delay your return home for a few 
days or a week. A return flight from Wellington to Tauranga wiould cost from 
around $NZ200 - 280, depending on when you travel. It is a 1 hour 10 minute 
flight and I can meet you at the airport.  You won't need to pay for 
accommodation :-)

Give me a couple of weeks warning if you can.

Hope to see you :-)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<a99836f1-f94b-4d8f-81de-2cb4375031d0@l7g2000vbv.googlegroups.com> <8on3qvF7b7U1@mid.individual.net> <ig7dbp$94t$1@reader1.panix.com> <7afba007-2483-49d0-8cdc-c7e856da8fcf@fj8g2000vbb.googlegroups.com> <ig81a8$hif$1@reader1.panix.com> <fabce8de-aafb-44d5-aa27-716a2f8d9bbf@f20g2000vbc.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 1/8/2011 7:24 AM, Alistair Maclean wrote:
> On Jan 7, 9:41 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> (snip)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pray tell, what is trn?

From:  http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rn_%28newsreader%29

"rn (short for Read News) is a news client (or 'newsreader') written by 
Larry Wall and originally released in 1984. It was one of the first 
newsreaders to take full advantage of character-addressable CRT 
terminals (vnews, by Kenneth Almquist was first). Previous newsreaders, 
such as readnews, were mostly line-oriented and designed for use on the 
printing terminals which were common on the early Unix minicomputers 
where the Usenet software and network originated. Later variants of the 
original rn program included rrn, trn, and strn.[1]"

After following some links in the article it seems that newer versions 
of trn (or its successors) may now support a mouse...but that doesn't 
mean DocDwarf's computer has a mouse.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Keys versus mouse was Re: OT Re: Aftershock

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-10T12:37:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ouv5aFcj4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<a99836f1-f94b-4d8f-81de-2cb4375031d0@l7g2000vbv.googlegroups.com> <8on3qvF7b7U1@mid.individual.net> <ig7dbp$94t$1@reader1.panix.com> <8opiflFpglU1@mid.individual.net> <iga5dc$hmt$3@reader1.panix.com> <8osg2rFiheU1@mid.individual.net> <hmcki619hnmljes2rj0j5272odralvstbc@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Sun, 9 Jan 2011 14:07:39 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> require less fine motor control.

While I respect and grant you the right to your opinion Clark, I vehemently 
dispute that you can page faster with Page Down than with a scroll 
gadget/scroll bar/mouse scroll.

I don't find motor control to be a problem, (the alcohol I consume hasn't 
affected the motor centres yet, although I'll grant that a mind which was 
once like a razor, now resembles a pair of rusty hedge clippers... :-))

Having read something through, I may want to re-read a certain part of it. 
Perhaps it is a thirty page document and I know the bit I want is around the 
middle. Are you seriously suggesting that 15 Page Downs/Page Ups is easier 
or faster than a single mouse click halfway down a scroll bar? (the scroll 
bar is an analogue of the document; some people don't realize that)

Sorry, I can't buy that.

For reading, I use the mouse; for writing, I use keys.

However, I accept that everyone works differently and have no problem with 
that.

Doc's challenge was specifically for Microsoft Outlook (NOT trn) which is 
Windows software DESIGNED to be used with a GUI interface.

Pete.
```

```
On Jan 10, 12:37 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Clark F Morris wrote:
> > On Sun, 9 Jan 2011 14:07:39 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> gadget/scroll bar/mouse scroll.

Testing on my screen I can hit the PageDn or PageUp key with a single
movement and the screen instantly shows the next page.

To use the scroll wheel moves the text at x lines/second and takes
longer than a single block move.

To use the scroll bar requires that I move the mouse to the bar (on,
above or below the grip) and then either click or click, hold, and
move an appropriate amount. These take longer than a single action of
hitting a key.

I don't any evidence about whether Clark scrolls faster with a key or
a mouse other than what he says, and nor do you.

It may well be that you (Peter) can use the mouse faster than the
keyboard, but your argument is that Clark can.


> I don't find motor control to be a problem, (the alcohol I consume hasn't
> affected the motor centres yet, although I'll grant that a mind which was
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> bar is an analogue of the document; some people don't realize that)

Now you are changing the argument to something else entirely. You are
comparing _15_ key hits with _one_ mouse move when the original was
one key hit vs one mouse move.

In any case you are mistaken 'a single mouse click halfway down a
scroll bar' does not put you halfway in the document (on any systems
that I have used), it put you one screenful closer to the middle
(unless you are on the handle where it does nothing).


> Sorry, I can't buy that.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that.

Except when you "vehemently dispute" of course.


> Doc's challenge was specifically for Microsoft Outlook (NOT trn) which is
> Windows software DESIGNED to be used with a GUI interface.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
```

```
Richard wrote:
> On Jan 11, 9:45 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> If it isn't a problem then why are you arguing about it ?

Like you, I can argue about anything I want to. I don't have to have any 
particular reason.

I have continued this correspondence to this point  because it appears to be 
of some importance to you.

And it is interesting to see how different people work.

However, I won't be posting further on it.


>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It may well be 'better' for you and _NOT_ be 'better' for Clark or me.

Sure, I said that.
>
> It might be 'better' for some situations, perhaps your "large
> documents", but not for the actual issue that was under discussion
> which was 'page down' be it single or five. You have changed the basis
> to provide a different argument.

I don't believe the original post said anything about how many pages were in 
the document.

Let me see...

Ah yes, here it is....

<quote>

>
> NO page downs for Outlook; it just scrolls smoothly.

No page downs for Outlook?  What happens when the cursor has been set in
the text and one presses the PgDn key (or, perhaps, the space bar)?

</quote>

Note: "...the cursor has been set IN THE TEXT..." (My emphasis) nothing to 
do with the number of pages.

I never sought to change the basis of the argument (as you accused me of). I 
used the example of a large document in my response to Clark.


>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> single 'pagedown' or five.

You are arguing with yourself. The ONLY point I disputed (and still do...) 
is that it is quicker to hit PageUp or PageDown, multiple times in a large 
document, than it is to use the mouse.

The rest of it is simply your spin for reasons of your own.

>
> Specifically your example (of clicking in the scroll bar to get to a
> specific place) does not work, and even if it did, you would need to
> know exactly where this was without searching back and forth.

And, in the Windows environment, it DOES work and the problem of knowing 
where you are is solved with a tooltip which MS Word obligingly pops up 
alongside the scroll bar, showing what page you are on.

I just tested it with a 30 page document. I can click and hold the middle of 
the scroll bar and watch the slider run to the point I clicked, with the 
page numbers showing as they run down or up. It stops on page 15, but I 
could release it to stop on any page before that.Similarly I can click on a 
point 2 thirds of the way down the scroll bar and have it run to page 20. It 
is an analogue of the document, as I mentioned earlier.

It probably works similarly with Paging keys, but I already noted why I 
don't like mixing keys and mouse if I don't have to.

Perhaps you should try a windows system running MS Word 2007 and see for 
yourself.

Perhaps, as a final statement (although I would have thought it was 
unnecessary)

"Opinions expressed in my posts are my own. Facts in my posts are facts and 
can be checked. I do not believe that the way I personally do things is the 
"only right" way and that everybody else should emulate it, neither do I 
believe people who do things other ways are "wrong" UNLESS I specifically 
said that in a post. I can dispute matters of fact or opinion without it 
being personal."

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:8on3qvF7b7U1@mid.individual.net...
> SkippyPB wrote:
<snip>
> if it comes off for you, see if you can delay your return home for a few 
> days or a week. A return flight from Wellington to Tauranga wiould cost 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
My favoritte US versus UK (and apparently NZ) language difference.

UK (NZ) - "return trip" (ticket) - (or return flight as above)
   =
US "round trip" (ticket)

  ****

US "return trip" (ticket)
  =
UK (NZ) "2nd half or a return trip" (ticket

  ***

I had just relocated to the UK to work for Micro Focus and had their travel 
department booking a flight back to the US for me (to attend a COBOL 
Standards meeting <G>) when they told me they booked a "return ticket" for 
me.  I really wondered how I had blown the job so quickly and WHY they were 
returning me to the US.  After discussion, we discovered this language 
difference.
```

```
LOL!

I can see your point. I guess when you say "return" you mean "return leg". 
We do say "round trip" sometimes and it means the same as "return trip".

Now that you mention it, the American usage is more logical.

How about this one:

In Australia, Sellotape (Scotch or Bear tape in the US) is called "durex". 
In NZ "durex" is a brand of condom.

You can imagine the looks from people in the office when the new Australian 
employee innocently asks: "Anyone got any durex?"

Pete.
```

```
posting it anywhere.  Be fore-warned though...New York is the
unattended public toilet of the world...but Jersey is its septic tank.

-----------------------

Location                                              : Wayne, NJ

accepted

Job Description: * The person should have knowledge of VMS along with
COBOL. VMS knowledge involves VMS commands, DCL,DECFORMS,etc. *
Experienced in working in support * Tasks in support: Incident
Management, Maintenance activities, monitoring jobs etc. * For both
support and maintenance a solid background of VMS/COBOL would be
required along with functional knowledge of the application.

Please submit following information along with resume.

Full Name of the Candidate
```

#### ↳ Re: The demise of COBOL and VMS is only talked about by those who know nothing

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-05T13:41:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c911e8e-dc68-4bcd-b605-cd2c9d1594d0@o9g2000pre.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<a214614b-a808-4c48-b539-365a8397abd4@q18g2000vbk.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jan 6, 6:20 am, seasoned_geek <rol...@logikalsolutions.com> wrote:

It seems that you are here just to argue and insult.

Initally you posted to promote your own book, you failed to mention
that it was self-promotion and provided an alleged quote from "one
college student" that was probably just quoting yourself.

This is a COBOL group we are all well aware that COBOL was once the
major language of businesses and that was a decade or three ago. While
COBOL may well continue for quite some time its importance will
decline.

Much the same can be said of VMS/OpenVMS. There may well be dozens,
hundreds, perhaps thousands of sites, but it is more likely that its
market share will decline rather than grow. HP is already offering
alternatives for the 'non-stop' market.

OpenVMS was sold on VAX and Alpha, both of which are dead, and on
Itanium and Itanium2 which don't have a wide acceptance and may follow
the other two into obsolescence.

To your specific points:

"it is the backbone for the Patriot Missile System"

That was 1975. Current information has:

"The WCC is the main computer within the Patriot system. It is a 24-
bit parallel militarized computer with fixed and floating point
capability. It is organized in a multiprocessor configuration that
operates at a maximum clock rate of 6 megahertz. This computer
controls the operator interface, calculates missile intercept
algorithms, and provides limited fault diagnostics. Compared to modern
personal computers, it has somewhat limited processing power, although
it has been upgraded several times during Patriot's service life."

That doesn't sound like VMS, but maybe it is.

"how many thousand ManMan installations are still out there"

ASK, who developed ManMan folded in 1996. The software went though
different hands, including CA, the place where software goes to die.
It re-emerged and ManMan/X which runs on Unix. There may be ManMan(/X)
installations out there but how many are still 15 year old OpenVMS
sites ?

"TicketMaster sells is sold and printed via OpenVMS."

Ticketmaster's databases ran on MS-SQLServer in the late 90s and then
converted to MySQL running on Linux in 2002.

Apparently the core Ticketmaster system was written in VAX Macro-32
assembler and now runs under Virtual Machines on Linux boxes. It needs
to be completely rewritten to go anywhere else.

"If you knew anything at all about MySQL you would know that it's a
limited and somewhat buggy wrapper around a multitude of storage
systems"

It seems to work fine for TicketMaster, one of your poster sites.

"While HP's UX division has been trying for a while to kill off
OpenVMS"

Exactly. If the company that owns it wants it dead then it will be.

"Once again you need to do research.  There have been 6 confirmed LSE
outages, but that was the largest."

"The replacement system is on Itanium."

That has nothing to do with OpenVMS and is not true.

"""MillenniumIT claims on its website that it has developed the
“world’s fastest” trading platform, running on Intel Xeon 5500
processors, and potentially delivering transaction times of 0.13
milliseconds.

The company has said it will be possible to process one million orders
per second “using volume produced Intel two socket Nehalem servers”,
within a commercially affordable environment."""

Xeon and Nehalem are not Itanium.

"> Singapore has been running successfully for one month on Itanium/
OpenVMS."

Can you support this with evidence ? It seems that Singapore is buying
INET running on Linux.

"""But only five days after its announcement the LSE faced a potential
contest when the New York-based NASDAQ exchange, whose Genium INET
platform runs on Linux in a reported C++ environment, said it had
delivered an average 97 microsecond latency during a week in mid-
October. In its Nordic markets, it said it was “capable” of continuing
to deliver sub-100 microsecond latency on average.

Importantly, however, NASDAQ is understood to quote 250 microseconds
to clients because of peak trading loads at the start and end of
trading days, a challenge for all markets.

The Singapore Stock Exchange, which is buying in the INET platform
from NASDAQ, has said in recent months it achieved 90 microsecond
times in benchmark testing, on Voltaire switches and HP ProLiant blade
servers. That system will go live next year and then accurate live
measurements can be taken."""

HP ProLiant use AMD Opteron or Intel Xeons and do not run OpenVMS.
```

```
In article <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>,
abrsvc  <dansabrservices@yahoo.com> wrote:

[snip]

>FWIW:  The job listing in the initial post I believe refers to a
>Transit System support application and has been posted in various job-
>boards for over a year.

I've seen such long-lived advertisements before; they strike me as odd.  
Once a pimp told me something along the lines of 'Everyone Knows that 
old-style COBOL programmers are a dime-a-dozen and these people are very 
particular about the kind of experience they want to see on paper before 
an interview'.

When I responded 'Ummmm... if they've been looking such a long time for 
someone one might think they'd have concluded that the kind of skills 
they want to see are *not* a dime-a-dozen and they might want to consider 
someone who has a different shoe-size or blood-type' the pimp made a 
strangled noise that sounded like 'heh heh heh'.

Happy, healthy, sane people just might, possibly, realise that if they 
keep asking for the same thing and don't find it they might want to change 
either what they are asking for or what they are satisfied with in 
response to their request.

>The current system runs under OpenVMS and is
>being ported to a Windows or Linux based system using VB.  Assistance
>is needed to support the legacy system until such time as the new
>system is complete.

So they want someone to 'sit in a closet' for (n) years, supporting a 
system for which they will no longer have use.  I wonder how much extra 
they pay for demanding that kind of stagnation.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The demise of COBOL and VMS is only talked about by those who know nothing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-08T11:24:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8opi4qFnhdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<a214614b-a808-4c48-b539-365a8397abd4@q18g2000vbk.googlegroups.com> <7c911e8e-dc68-4bcd-b605-cd2c9d1594d0@o9g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <8okrfnFer1U1@mid.individual.net> <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <ig81u8$54$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article
> <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> response to their request.

Or, perhaps, increase the incentive for making an application... :-)
>
>> The current system runs under OpenVMS and is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> they pay for demanding that kind of stagnation.

Sometimes handling Legacy CAN be interesting. I remember one particular 
project with some affection. The hardest part for me personally, (and the 
most rewarding) was ensuring that the 21 people on my Legacy team were 
re-absorbed into the organization or retrained for the new Siebel system. 
All but 2 were salvaged and they didn't want to do the 20 mile commute that 
would have been necessary to keep their jobs.  I spent a lot of time 
lobbying managers to pick up the Legacy team people (who, of course, all had 
really good business knowledge). A senior management decision had been taken 
that Siebel would decide who worked on the new system and they apparently 
didn't want anyone with knowledge of the old system.I never understood the 
reason for that and, in the end, I managed to get 2 of the team onto the new 
project with suitable training.

It is a real managment challenge to keep people motivated and interested in 
coming to work when you are not allowed to spend any money on the "old" 
system and everybody knows their future is uncertain...Nevertheless, it was 
achieved and they threw an amazing party for me (which I still remember with 
fondness) when the project was completed.

The best way to handle the stagnation is by paying contractors to do it.  (I 
was brought in with the brief to "keep legacy running until we cut over", on 
the basis that I had done some previous successful projects at the same 
company.) I found a de-motivated and disspirited team who had suffered from 
some poor management, and were generally perceived by the Busness as "the 
Enemy" :-). When I left, their KPIs were the highest they had ever been and 
their perception in the company was completely changed.  (In fact, some of 
the Business managers were questioning why the Siebel system was being 
implemented when the current IT service was now so good...)

I honestly don't know how successful the new Siebel system was but it cost 
24 million and the IT budget for the Legacy system was around 9 million per 
year.

(I should add that the Legacy was NOT a COBOL system; it was all SQL 
procedures. There were some very talented people working on it.)

Pete.
```

```
In article <8opi4qFnhdU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

[snip]

>> So they want someone to 'sit in a closet' for (n) years, supporting a
>> system for which they will no longer have use.  I wonder how much
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>re-absorbed into the organization or retrained for the new Siebel system. 

I would argue, Mr Dashwood, that ensuring the re-absorption or retraining 
of the personnel involved is *not* 'handling legacy', it is making sure 
that Corner Office Idiots don't get their way when they say 'This person 
has been with us for (n) years and expects to earn a decent living based 
on their knowledge of our particular organisation and the skills they've 
demonstrated, repeatedly, in showing how they can maintain profit... but 
we can get *two* new graduates for less than the cost of one of them'.

>All but 2 were salvaged and they didn't want to do the 20 mile commute that 
>would have been necessary to keep their jobs.  I spent a lot of time 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>project with suitable training.

The reasoning, as far as I can understand it, is very simple.  Managers or 
Executives get to abdicate their responsibilites completely and an outside 
organisation does the dirty work.  If it works then Managers or Executives 
take the credit, if it doesn't... well, them durned outsiders don't know 
that Things Are Different Here.

>
>It is a real managment challenge to keep people motivated and interested in 
>coming to work when you are not allowed to spend any money on the "old" 
>system and everybody knows their future is uncertain...

The textbooks I've read disagree with that 'real management challenge', Mr 
Dashwood; they tend to say things like 'The allocation, co-ordination and 
motivation of personnel and resources towards the accomplishment of a 
stated Executive goal is the job of Management'.

Not 'when you are not allowed to spend any money on the "old" system', not 
'when times are good and stock-options are lavished on everyone'... at 
*all* times keeping folks motivated is Management's job.  That's what 
they're paid for, if they want chocolates and roses too just for doing 
their jobs... well, they're Management and can reward themselves with 
such.

[snip]

>The best way to handle the stagnation is by paying contractors to do it.

That's what I've noticed in the advertisements and I believe I 
mentioned... 'sit in the closet and stagnate, technically, until we're 
ready to toss you out'.  I still wonder how much extra they pay for those 
supposedly 'sharpest knives' that contractors are expected to be ('that's 
why *you're* paid The Big Bucks' and all that) for the contractors to 
ignore what should be their innate qualities.

DD
```

- **References:** `<a214614b-a808-4c48-b539-365a8397abd4@q18g2000vbk.googlegroups.com> <7c911e8e-dc68-4bcd-b605-cd2c9d1594d0@o9g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <8okrfnFer1U1@mid.individual.net> <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <8opgmgFethU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sat, 8 Jan 2011 10:59:43 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>abrsvc wrote:
>> On Jan 5, 10:33 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
…[163 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.

That doesn't necessarily indicate that COBOL is dying or there are no
jobs out there.  It shows me two things:  1)  Universities and tech
schools quit teaching COBOL believing the media hype that COBOL was an
ancient unneeded language and all of those million (maybe billion)
lines of COBOL code were going to disappear and be replaced by (insert
the language soup du jour of your choice here); 2)  Old geezers, like
myself, are not retiring because there are jobs in COBOL available and
companies are willing to spend money to keep those that are proficient
in it whether that person be an employee or a contractor.

My own employer, desperate to put a plan in place to replace all of us
baby boomers who will begin retiring in 2011 and onward, has
reinstituted a program they once had 15 years ago.  They are
recruiting college grads who don't necessarily have computer degrees
but have math and/or business accounting degrees, bringing them in,
teaching them COBOL and teaching them our applications.

COBOL may someday be relegated to the Smithsonian, but not in my
lifetime or, possibly, anyone's in this newsgroup.

Regards,
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<igaoub$3ch$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a214614b-a808-4c48-b539-365a8397abd4@q18g2000vbk.googlegroups.com> <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <8opgmgFethU1@mid.individual.net> <qm5hi6t59vfmmgdv2e73vij0b2etbufo8k@4ax.com>`

```
In article <qm5hi6t59vfmmgdv2e73vij0b2etbufo8k@4ax.com>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

[snip]

>My own employer, desperate to put a plan in place to replace all of us
>baby boomers who will begin retiring in 2011 and onward, has
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>teaching them COBOL and teaching them our applications.

As the Germans say, 'plus ca change, plus c'est la meme chose'.  When I 
search the UseNet archives using http://www.groups.google.com for the word 
'crackerjack' (no ') in comp.lang.cobol I get only one hit for a 
re-posting I made in 2003... but the URL I included for the original in 
1997 is there and it still seems to hold.  From 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/6c29c3f3dc17173c?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text:

Years ago the Wall Street Journal did a story on one of the major NY 
houses... I think it was Morgan Stanley or Morgan Guaranty or the like. 
They hired *only* the 'unhireable'... kids with BAs in Library Science, 
Art History, etc... they put these kids through two years of hell, 60 - 70 
hr weeks, and turned them into *crackerjack* programmers... and then saw 
said kids being hired away by the competition at double or triple the 
salary.  When asked why a raise did not accompany the completion of the 
course the HR representative replied 'Oh, we cannot do that... all the 
money has been taken up by training.' (compare this with 'Oh, we cannot 
upgrade the door-locks... all the money went into oil-paintings to hang on 
the walls.')

After a few years of seeing themselves serve as Wall Street's unofficial 
programming school the company finally 'wised up'... and cancelled the 
program entirely.

--end quoted text

(oh... there's also something in that same posting, earlier, about what 
Management's responsibility is)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The demise of COBOL and VMS is only talked about by those who know nothing

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2011-01-09T13:58:06+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8osfgvFfulU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<a214614b-a808-4c48-b539-365a8397abd4@q18g2000vbk.googlegroups.com> <7c911e8e-dc68-4bcd-b605-cd2c9d1594d0@o9g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <8okrfnFer1U1@mid.individual.net> <846cb739-6e63-4b23-b756-0f13580c9be4@j29g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <8opgmgFethU1@mid.individual.net> <qm5hi6t59vfmmgdv2e73vij0b2etbufo8k@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
<snip>
>> Over the last 3 decades I have watched the COBOL jobs posted on
>> Jobserve (UK) decline from hundreds to a handful. Whether we WANT it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> jobs out there.

I think it does, Steve.

And I decided, as it is a quiet Sunday afternoon here to take sme time out 
and see if, one more time, I can explain why COBOL is in decline. (I'm 
getting tired of people saying it is because of "fashion" or "media hype"... 
it isn't. There are sound reasons for it that make sense when a broader view 
is taken.)

If people can't get employed with a particular language skill, that skill 
will eventually become obsolete, or of interest only to Academics studying 
the history of computing.

While it will be some time yet before there are NO COBOL jobs  you surely 
wouldn't deny that the prospects for a COBOL programmer in 1975 and the 
prospects for such a programmer today are significantly diminished?

(I was working as a free-lance COBOL programmer in 1975 and the future was 
bright...now I work mostly in C# and it is even brighter...)


> It shows me two things:  1)  Universities and tech
> schools quit teaching COBOL believing the media hype that COBOL was an
> ancient unneeded language and all of those million (maybe billion)
> lines of COBOL code were going to disappear and be replaced by (insert
> the language soup du jour of your choice here);

Universities CREATE media hype. (Well, to be fair, the media keep an eye on 
what is happening in Universities because it is often newsworthy.)

Nobody moved off COBOL because of hype. An entrenched community who had 
become reactionary and arrogant refused to change or adopt to a new 
paradigm, believing that the way they had always done things was the only 
way.

In it's heyday (60s through to end of the 70s) COBOL WAS the only game in 
town and "computer programming" was a mysterious Black Art that the general 
public regarded with awe and suspicion. The Priests Of COBOL protected their 
turf and new approaches were generally dismissed and ridiculed. Nobody 
believed there was any need to change. Nobody believed that anyone else 
(outside the Priesthood) could know anything about computer programming. 
Anything new was evaluated in terms of COBOL and immediately shown to be 
lacking. I saw it time and again across sites, countries, and continents; 
and know whereof  I speak.

But the rest of the world wasn't in the Priesthood. They weren't even True 
Believers. They just wanted to be able to make computers do what they 
wanted.

IBM (which was the major Faith at the time) shot itself in the foot by 
inventing a very cheap personal computer that was within the reach of 
everyone. It very nearly cost them the company.  You can clearly trace the 
decline of COBOL from the arrival of the PC in the early 1980s. A PC on its 
own was fun but not really a serious contender for commercial processing 
(and they were mainly programmed in BASIC at the time, which was still 
interpreted rather than compiled.) I can remember a certain programmer 
sniffing when he heard I had obtained a Commodore Pet: "Don't know why you 
waste your time with toy computers, you have access to the real thing..." 
(He gave up on me completely when I showed him my Sinclair ZX Spectrum 
playing casino Blackjack a few years later :-))

But as soon as you started connecting them, something magical happened. The 
synergy obtained was far more than anyone had expected. The Military 
(ARPANet), Academia (JANET) and Commerce (CyberNet, Dial 360...) all started 
playing with networked computers and it had impact on the way applications 
were developed. A language intended for a centralized processor with 
complete  sequential control of all operations (like COBOL) wasn't good 
enough. Hardware was advancing in huge leaps and it wasn't going to sit 
around waiting for software to attend to it. A new term started to be 
heard... "interrupt driven". Instead of software controlling hardware, the 
hardware simply demanded attention and expected softwere to service it. This 
was a completely new paradigm to COBOL programmers, who shrugged and 
dismissed it as "unnecessary".

But Networking wasn't going to go away and a new programming paradigm was 
invented to support it: Object Orientation. Encapsulated chunks of 
intelligent code which were not dependent on a single centralized processor, 
could be activated by interrupts, and could be transferred  as "objects" 
across systems to run where required in real time, started showing that 
applications didn't have to be huge, monolithic, core images compiled from 
thousands of lines of code. Functionality could be "distributed" and reused. 
The concept of component based applications evolved and Object Orientation 
was the perfect way to implement it.

COBOL was upgraded to support OO but most of the community were not 
interested and couldn't see the point. "It's just Modular Programming 
re-invented...".  It wasn't.  I tried on numerous occasions here to get 
people to recognise the subtle differences that had such HUGE implications 
(ITSA and ITSLIKE), and finally gave it up.

Java arrived. It was regarded with hostility by COBOL people who simply saw 
it taking their jobs. When Java projects failed there was glee in the COBOL 
camp. (When COBOL projects failed it was the fault of Management...) Again, 
I said that COBOL people should expand their skill sets to include Java. 
Some did and are still employed today; others didn't, and aren't.

Coming back to your point, Steve: NOBODY in Academia believed that the 
existing investment in COBOL code would be replaced with the "Language du 
Jour". People in Academia, simply DON'T CARE about that; it is the preserve 
of Commerce to decide what they will do about it. Academia is more concerned 
with developing and evolving better approaches, not with what happens to the 
old ones.

The famous "Billions of Lines" claimed by Gartner (which they subsequently 
revised and which they no longer quote) HAS been eroded by languages like 
Java, C#, VB.Net, and even C++. But, more importantly, scripting languages 
like Python and Ruby are cutting into what was once the preserve of COBOL. 
ALL of the modern languages are completely comfortable with the OO paradigm 
and deployment on the Network. COBOL just isn't.

Over a period of years the World's existing COBOL codebase WILL be 
refactored, replaced, and discarded. (the reasons I am confident of this 
would take a completely separate article, but believe it I do...I should 
also note in passing that I am currently employed in making this happen, so 
I WOULD say that :-))

 2)  Old geezers, like
> myself, are not retiring because there are jobs in COBOL available and
> companies are willing to spend money to keep those that are proficient
> in it whether that person be an employee or a contractor.

Sure. As long as there are people around who can cost/effectively keep it 
going, it makes sense to use them. And, to be fair, there are still some 
commecrial applications (like batch processing) where COBOL is probably the 
best tool for the job. (I don't believe Batch Processing will be with us for 
ever though, and again, my reasons for believing that would require a 
separate article.)
>
> My own employer, desperate to put a plan in place to replace all of us
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> teaching them COBOL and teaching them our applications.

That's what they're doing right now. How about in 5 years... 10 years?

Those graduates know more. They are young, bright, people. Their friends are 
young, bright people. They talk. As they move up the ladder they will press 
for something better. The applications which are perfectly adequate today, 
may not always be. If the industry you are working in is competitive change 
is often driven by competitive advantage. If the competitor shows they can 
respond to market opportunities more quickly  (all they have to do is write 
a script), while your company has to prepare a spec get it reviewed, have it 
programmed, debugged, and acceptance tested, along with all the regression 
testing for things that might have been affected....well, you catch my 
drift. COBOL is not just a Language "problem" it is a system development 
methodology problem as well.

Objects are encapsulated. So immediately, regression testing is dramatically 
reduced or rendered completely unnecessary. Objects are small so they are 
easier to change and and can be rewritten completely, much more easily than 
a monolithic COBOL program. Objects can be reused, so your competitior can 
simply change the way components are connected instead of needing to 
undertake a full system review before changing anything. The list goes on, 
but the world has voted with its feet. COBOL is NOT declining because it is 
inherently a BAD language. It is happening because there are better 
alternatives available, and, in particular, the Obect paradigm supports 
networking better, and networking provides competitive advantage to 
commerce.

>
> COBOL may someday be relegated to the Smithsonian, but not in my
> lifetime or, possibly, anyone's in this newsgroup.

Well, I guess that depends to a large extend on how long you intend to live 
:-)

For many commercial sites it has ALREADY been "relegated to the Smithsonian" 
inasmuch as they no longer use it or have any interest in it.

In the end time will tell.

I see no reason to retract or revise my 1995 estimate of 2015 as being the 
time when COBOL will no longer be the development language of choice. 
(Actually, I think that is already the case, but by 2015 most people will 
see it to be so.)

BOTTOM LINE:

1. COBOL is not declining because of Media hype.
2. Universities are not teaching COBOL because they think it is an "old" 
language (some of them still teach FORTRAN). They are simply aware that 
there are better solutions available.
3. The reasons for the decline of COBOL are complex and manifold. (COBOL 
shop attitudes, standards taking 17 years to approve, conservatism, 
ostrichism, smugness, increasing cost of labour, increasing cost of 
development... all of these things could be argued to have contributed. But 
the main single reason was the replacement of the procedural paradigm with 
the Object Oriented one, in concordance with the rise of distributed 
(networked) processing over centralised. This, more than anything else, 
rendered COBOL redundant.

Pete.
```
